summary:
Between approximately 4100 and 3400 BCE, the [[event:38|Cucuteni-Trypillia culture]] of present-day Ukraine and Moldova created some of the largest human settlements in the world — settlements now referred to by archaeologists as mega-sites. These extraordinary communities covered between 100 and 320 hectares and may have housed populations of 10,000 to 15,000 individuals or more, making them significantly larger than contemporary Mesopotamian urban centers and among the largest agglomerations of people anywhere in the world at that time.

The mega-sites are characterized by a distinctive concentric ring layout visible from aerial and satellite photography. Houses were arranged in multiple concentric oval or circular rows around a central open space, with up to three or four rings of structures at the largest sites. Individual houses were large rectangular structures, sometimes two stories high, built from wattle-and-daub over wooden frames. Houses within a single ring appear to have been built according to consistent plans and may have been constructed collectively by the community. Excavations have revealed that many of these houses were deliberately burned at the end of their occupation — a puzzling ritual practice whose meaning remains debated.

Despite their impressive size, the Trypillia mega-sites show relatively little evidence of the social hierarchy that characterized contemporaneous Mesopotamian cities. There are no obvious palace structures, no monumental temples on raised platforms, and burials do not show extreme wealth differentiation. This has led some scholars to describe Trypillia society as a form of heterarchical or corporate community organization, in which collective decision-making and communal identity took precedence over individual or dynastic authority. How communities of ten thousand or more people coordinated without the administrative infrastructure typical of early cities remains an open question.

Sustaining large populations in one location placed significant demands on the surrounding agricultural landscape. Trypillia communities practiced a slash-and-burn or shifting cultivation system, periodically relocating settlements as soils in the immediate vicinity became exhausted. This agricultural strategy explains both the apparently short occupation spans of individual mega-sites (typically 50–150 years) and the density of settlement remains across the region — as communities moved, they left behind dense archaeological signatures. The mega-site phenomenon therefore represents an organizational solution to the challenge of feeding large populations in a temperate woodland environment without deep-irrigation agriculture.

The Trypillia mega-sites challenge narratives of civilization that equate urban scale with social stratification and administrative complexity. They demonstrate that very large human communities could exist in prehistoric Europe organized on principles quite different from those of Near Eastern city-states, raising fundamental questions about the range of social forms through which complex societies can develop.
